text: The surgeon places ports in a straight line starting from the left upper quadrant, spaced eight centimeters apart, extending towards the right lower quadrant.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to switch the transverse colon and right colon to the abdominal wall, enhancing exposure and freeing the instruments for dissection.

The surgeon opens the mesenteric envelope and dissects the tumors using a medial-lateral approach. The duodenum is exposed, and the ileocolic vessels are identified. The ileocolic vein, branching from the superior mesenteric vein, is clipped and divided. The distal ileum is identified, and the mesentery is divided to address pelvic adhesions, freeing the small intestine for subsequent anastomosis.

The surgeon uses the fourth robotic arm to continue the cephalad dissection, identifying the right colic vein, which is clipped and divided using robotic clips. The right colic artery is then immediately identified, clipped, and divided without the use of laparoscopic-assisted instruments.

The surgeon dissects over the pancreas and duodenum, demonstrating close proximity to these structures. The suture is taken down for further retraction using the fourth arm. The right branch of the midcolic vessels is identified, clipped, and divided.

A sponge is placed underneath the transverse colon, which is then pulled caudally to enter the lesser sac. The surgeon divides the omentum and identifies the right gastroepiploic vessels along the greater curvature of the stomach, which are subsequently divided. The dissection progresses towards the hepatic flexure.

The surgeon identifies the gastrocolic trunk and connects the spaces from the previous dissection, continuing towards the hepatic flexure to fully mobilize the right colon. The previous suture is utilized to relieve tension during this process.
The surgeon divides the distal ileum and marks it with a suture for future reference. The transverse colon is also divided, and any remaining attachments are completely severed using a vessel sealer.

The surgeon places the disconnected specimen aside. The distal ileum is positioned close to the transverse colon. The anastomosis is parked in the right upper quadrant, with a suture placed and the fourth arm used for optimal exposure.

The surgeon performs an isoperistaltic anastomosis between the small intestine and transverse colon. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to assess the blood supply to both structures. A 45mm stapler is then utilized to complete the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses the robotic fourth arm to align the anastomosis between the distal ileum and transverse colon. The common channel opening is closed with a 3-0 absorbable barbed suture, employing a canal suture technique.
The completed anastomosis is visible, with the barbed suture locked on itself. The surgeon uses ICG to check the blood supply of the anastomosis, confirming proper perfusion. All sutures are removed.
